risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were randomised with a computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomisation), with additional context of stratification by centre. The mention of block size does not undermine randomness. Therefore the allocation sequence was very likely truly random.

Quote: "At each centre, the person responsible for inclusion emailed the research unit, presented the participant’s project ID number, name and gender before treatment allocation was disclosed. The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete." Comment: This describes a central allocation process via the research unit, with treatment assignment disclosed only after the participant was enrolled and identifying information submitted. The allocation code was withheld from recruiters/clinicians, preventing foreknowledge of upcoming assignments.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After random allocation, participants were informed about the outcome, and the intervention group was given a date for its first session." Quote: "Patients allocated to the control group received standard treatment from their GP, national insurance office (NAV), other health professionals, and received a letter with information and encouragement to use available services and self-help resources." Quote: "The secondary outcome measures were questionnaire-based changes in psychological distress, and symptoms of anxiety and depression by use of the Hospital Anxiety and Depression (HAD) Scale. EQ5D was used to measure changes in health-related quality of life. Self-report questionnaires were administered to the patients at baseline and after 6 and 12 months." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were not blinded in this pragmatic psychotherapy/job-support versus usual care trial; allocation was disclosed and the nature of the interventions makes blinding of personnel implausible. Because the study included subjective self-reported secondary outcomes (psychological distress, anxiety, depression, quality of life) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of assignment, the overall performance bias judgement is High risk, despite the primary work-participation outcome being registry-based and objective.

Quote: "The secondary outcome measures were questionnaire-based changes in psychological distress, and symptoms of anxiety and depression by use of the Hospital Anxiety and Depression (HAD) Scale. EQ5D was used to measure changes in health-related quality of life. Self-report questionnaires were administered to the patients at baseline and after 6 and 12 months." Quote: "After random allocation, participants were informed about the outcome, and the intervention group was given a date for its first session." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient self-report for secondary outcomes (HADS, EQ5D). Participants were informed of allocation, so these assessors were unblinded. Because these are subjective self-reported outcomes, their measurement could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although the primary work-participation outcome came from registry data and data analysts were blinded, the study also included unblinded subjective self-reported outcomes; by the worst-outcome rule, this domain is High risk.

Quote: "Data on the main outcome measure, work participation, were complete for all participants. However, for secondary outcomes based on self-report, 740 (62%) participants at 6 months follow-up and 636 (52%) participants at 12 months follow-up returned their questionnaires" and Table 3 reports 12-month Ns of "AWaC 376" and "Control 251" for secondary outcomes. Comment: For the primary work-participation outcome there was no post-randomization missing data, so attrition bias is low for that outcome. However, for the self-reported secondary outcomes at 12 months only 636/1193 (about 53%) provided data, i.e. about 47% missing overall, which exceeds the threshold for serious attrition. The reported analysed Ns by arm (376/630=59.7% vs 251/563=44.6%) indicate substantial differential missingness of about 15 percentage points between groups. Although inverse probability weighting was used, the combination of very high overall loss and marked imbalance across arms provides positive evidence that incomplete outcome data could bias the secondary outcome results.
